sections.header: Verses 1-5 — Request for Prayer and Confidence in the Lord

heb.paragraph: Paul asks prayer that the word of the Lord may trechō (v.1) — 'run' or 'spread rapidly.' The athletic metaphor pictures the gospel as a runner racing through the world. Prayer fuels the race.
The word should be doxazō (v.1) — 'glorified' or 'honored.' The gospel's advance brings glory to God; its reception is its vindication.
Deliver us from atopos (v.2) — 'evil' or 'perverse' — people. The word literally means 'out of place' — people whose behavior is disordered, who don't fit the moral order.
The Lord is pistos (v.3) — 'faithful.' Human faith wavers; divine faithfulness does not. God will strengthen and protect them from the evil one.

refs.note: Paul similarly requests prayer for an open door for the word in Colossians. Missionary work requires intercessory support.
Paul asks Roman believers to pray for deliverance from unbelievers in Judea and for successful ministry. Prayer is consistently linked to mission.

notes.note: Prayer for the word's advance is missional intercession. The gospel runs when churches pray. Ministry is not self-powered but prayer-powered.
Not everyone has faith. Paul is realistic about opposition. The gospel is both accepted and rejected; deliverance from opposers is a legitimate prayer request.
The Lord is faithful. In contrast to unreliable humans, God keeps his promises. This faithfulness is the ground for confidence that he will strengthen and protect.
Paul's confidence in them is actually confidence 'in the Lord' regarding them. Pastoral optimism is theologically grounded: God is at work in his people.
Calvin notes that even apostles need prayer. The most gifted minister depends on the church's intercession. Ministry is corporate, not individual heroism.
God's faithfulness is contrasted with human faithlessness. Calvin emphasizes that 'the Lord is faithful' is not merely description but assurance: we can rely on him.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'spread rapidly' (trechē) pictures the word as an athlete running a race. The prayer is for swift progress, not obstacles.
There is a wordplay: 'not everyone has faith' (pistis) but 'the Lord is faithful' (pistos). Human faith fails; divine faithfulness does not.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1-2 Thessalonians, NICNT (2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee notes the dual request: positive (word runs and is glorified) and negative (deliverance from opponents). Paul's mission requires both advance and protection.
Fee sees these verses as transitional, building confidence before the rebuke of idleness. Paul establishes relational capital before delivering hard words.

wanamaker.source: Charles A. Wanamaker, 1-2 Thessalonians, NIGTC (1990) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wanamaker observes that 'be glorified' (doxazētai) parallels 'run' (trechē) — both are subjunctives expressing Paul's prayer-wish. The word's rapid advance is its glorification.
The phrase 'not everyone has faith' is litotes (understatement). Paul means: some actively oppose. This is realistic assessment, not pessimism.

hist.context: Paul requests prayer for his ongoing mission. The gospel continues to face opposition — 'evil and perverse people' — but Paul is confident in God's faithfulness. The transitional verses (vv.4-5) prepare for the main concern of the chapter: idleness among some Thessalonians.

sections.header: Verses 6-12 — If Anyone Is Not Willing to Work, Let Them Not Eat

heb.paragraph: Some walk ataktōs (vv.6, 11) — 'idly' or 'disorderly.' The adverb derives from military vocabulary: soldiers who break ranks. These believers have abandoned their posts, refusing to work.
The verb peripateō (v.11) — 'walk' or 'live' — is Paul's standard term for lifestyle. How one 'walks' reveals one's character.
They are not busy (ergazomenous) but periergazomenous (v.11) — 'busybodies.' The wordplay is pointed: not working, but working around — meddling instead of producing.
They should work with hēsychia (v.12) — 'quietness' — and eat their own bread. The word suggests tranquility, minding one's own affairs without disruption.

refs.note: Paul's earlier instruction to 'work with your hands' anticipated this problem. The situation has evidently worsened, requiring more direct intervention.
Paul's instruction that the thief 'must work, doing something useful' reflects the same work ethic. Productivity, not idleness, characterizes the Christian life.

notes.note: Paul commands withdrawal from the idle 'in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ.' This is apostolic authority, not mere advice. The church must discipline its disorderly members.
Paul himself is the example. Though he had the right to support (as an apostle), he worked to provide a model. Leaders must embody what they teach.
The famous rule: 'If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat.' Note: not unable but unwilling. This is not heartlessness toward the disabled but confrontation of the lazy.
The wordplay — 'not busy but busybodies' — captures the problem. Idleness breeds meddling. Those who won't work find time to interfere in others' affairs.
The command is gentle yet firm: 'we urge and exhort.' Paul's tone combines pastoral concern with apostolic authority.
Calvin defends the rule as reasonable: those who can work but won't should face consequences. Charity is for the genuinely needy, not those who exploit generosity.
Busybodies are worse than idle. Calvin observes that those who refuse their own duties often meddle in others'. Productive work prevents troublemaking.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The condition 'if anyone is not willing to work' (ou thelei ergazesthai) emphasizes volition. The rule applies to those who refuse, not those who cannot.

The wordplay — mēden ergazomenous alla periergazomenous ('not working but overworking [in others' affairs]') — is untranslatable but effective. The idle are paradoxically very busy — just not productively.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1-2 Thessalonians, NICNT (2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee notes Paul's personal example is central to the argument. He worked 'night and day' (v.8) not because he had to but to model responsible living. Apostolic example is apostolic teaching.
Fee emphasizes the qualifying phrase: 'not willing.' Paul is not condemning the unemployed or disabled but those who could work and refuse. Context matters.

wanamaker.source: Charles A. Wanamaker, 1-2 Thessalonians, NIGTC (1990) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wanamaker analyzes 'keep away from' (stellesthai) — a strong term suggesting social distancing. The community pressure is meant to prompt repentance.
The wordplay is Pauline rhetoric at its best. Wanamaker notes that meddling (periergazomenous) may include spreading the eschatological confusion addressed in chapter 2.

hist.context: The idleness problem, hinted at in 1 Thess 4:11-12, now receives direct confrontation. Some Thessalonians had stopped working — perhaps expecting Christ's imminent return. Why work if the end is near? Paul's response is sharp: 'If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat' (v.10). This is not cruelty but tough love for a church tolerating irresponsibility.

heb.paragraph: Do not enkakeō (v.13) — 'grow weary' or 'lose heart' — in doing good. The verb suggests becoming exhausted or discouraged. Perseverance in good works requires ongoing energy.
They should sēmeioō (v.14) — 'take note of' or 'mark' — the disobedient. The verb suggests identifying for purposes of corrective action, not vindictive shaming.
They should noutheteō (v.15) — 'warn' or 'admonish' — the disobedient as brothers, not enemies. Discipline aims at restoration, not exclusion.

refs.note: Paul similarly urges, 'Let us not become weary in doing good, for at the proper time we will reap a harvest if we do not give up.'
Paul's instruction to 'not associate' with immoral believers provides a parallel to the discipline commanded here.

notes.note: Don't grow weary in doing good. The faithful majority must not let the idle minority discourage them. Persevere regardless of others' failures.
Taking note of the disobedient is not gossip but discernment. The community must identify those who refuse correction so that appropriate measures can be taken.
Treat them as brothers, not enemies. Discipline is familial, not judicial. The goal is restoration, not punishment. A brother who errs is still a brother.
The Lord of peace grants peace 'at all times and in every way.' Peace is comprehensive — not just absence of conflict but wholeness in all circumstances.
Paul's own handwriting authenticates the letter. Given the reference to a forged letter (2:2), this signature is particularly significant.
Calvin stresses the distinction between brother and enemy. Church discipline treats the wayward as family members who need correction, not foreigners to be expelled. Harshness defeats the purpose.
The signature is a mark of authenticity. Calvin notes that false teachers circulated fraudulent letters; Paul's autograph is his guarantee.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb 'take note of' (sēmeiousthe) is middle voice, suggesting personal involvement: 'note for yourselves.' The community must identify the disobedient without formal denunciation.
The 'distinguishing mark' (sēmeion) is Paul's handwriting. Ancient letters were typically dictated; the sender's personal touch authenticated them.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1-2 Thessalonians, NICNT (2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee emphasizes the balance: social pressure (do not associate) combined with brotherly concern (warn as a brother). Discipline without love becomes punitive; love without discipline becomes permissive.
Fee notes the poignancy: Paul's own hand writes the final greeting, making the letter unmistakably his. This personal touch contrasts with the impersonal forged letter.

wanamaker.source: Charles A. Wanamaker, 1-2 Thessalonians, NIGTC (1990) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wanamaker analyzes the discipline process: identification (take note), social consequences (do not associate), purpose (shame leading to repentance). The sequence is pastoral, not punitive.
The title 'Lord of peace' (ho kyrios tēs eirēnēs) echoes 'God of peace' in 1 Thess 5:23. Peace is the Lord's gift to a church troubled by confusion and idleness.

hist.context: Paul closes with general exhortations and specific instructions about discipline. The goal is restoration ('warn them as a brother'), not excommunication. The letter's authenticity marker — Paul's own handwritten greeting (v.17) — may counter the forged letter mentioned in 2:2.

rows.label: vv.1-5
rows.text: Prayer request and confidence in God's faithfulness

rows.label: vv.6-12
rows.text: Warning against idleness: Paul's example; the work rule

lit.note: Chapter 3 addresses practical community life. Paul requests prayer for his mission (vv.1-5), then confronts idleness sharply: 'If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat' (v.10). His own example of self-supporting labor is the model (vv.7-9). The letter closes with instructions for discipline (vv.14-15) and the authenticating signature (v.17) that distinguishes this letter from the forged one mentioned in 2:2.

themes.note: Chapter 3 emphasizes holiness (the call to orderly, productive living), mission (prayer for the gospel's advance), and justice (the fairness of the work rule and the discipline process). Faith appears in the transition (vv.3-4) as the ground of Paul's confidence.

hebtext.note: Paul asks prayer that the word of the Lord may trechō (v.1) — 'run' or 'spread rapidly.' The athletic metaphor pictures the gospel as a runner racing through the world. Prayer fuels the race.
The word should be doxazō (v.1) — 'glorified' or 'honored.' The gospel's advance brings glory to God; its reception is its vindication.
Deliver us from atopos (v.2) — 'evil' or 'perverse' — people. The word literally means 'out of place' — people whose behavior is disordered, who don't fit the moral order.
The Lord is pistos (v.3) — 'faithful.' Human faith wavers; divine faithfulness does not. God will strengthen and protect them from the evil one.
Some walk ataktōs (vv.6, 11) — 'idly' or 'disorderly.' The adverb derives from military vocabulary: soldiers who break ranks. These believers have abandoned their posts, refusing to work.
The verb peripateō (v.11) — 'walk' or 'live' — is Paul's standard term for lifestyle. How one 'walks' reveals one's character.
They are not busy (ergazomenous) but periergazomenous (v.11) — 'busybodies.' The wordplay is pointed: not working, but working around — meddling instead of producing.
They should work with hēsychia (v.12) — 'quietness' — and eat their own bread. The word suggests tranquility, minding one's own affairs without disruption.
Do not enkakeō (v.13) — 'grow weary' or 'lose heart' — in doing good. The verb suggests becoming exhausted or discouraged. Perseverance in good works requires ongoing energy.
They should sēmeioō (v.14) — 'take note of' or 'mark' — the disobedient. The verb suggests identifying for purposes of corrective action, not vindictive shaming.
They should noutheteō (v.15) — 'warn' or 'admonish' — the disobedient as brothers, not enemies. Discipline aims at restoration, not exclusion.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Some Thessalonians had stopped working — perhaps expecting Christ's imminent return…

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Prayer for the word's advance is missional intercession. The gospel runs when churches pray. Ministry is not self-powered but prayer-powered."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin notes that even apostles need prayer. The most gifted minister depends on the church's intercession. Ministry is corporate, not individual heroism."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The phrase 'spread rapidly' (trechē) pictures the word as an athlete running a race. The prayer is for swift progress, not obstacles."

thread.text: Paul similarly requests prayer for an open door for the word in Colossians. Missionary work requires intercessory support.
Paul asks Roman believers to pray for deliverance from unbelievers in Judea and for successful ministry. Prayer is consistently linked to mission.
Paul's earlier instruction to 'work with your hands' anticipated this problem. The situation has evidently worsened, requiring more direct intervention.
Paul's instruction that the thief 'must work, doing something useful' reflects the same work ethic. Productivity, not idleness, characterizes the Christian life.
Paul similarly urges, 'Let us not become weary in doing good, for at the proper time we will reap a harvest if we do not give up.'
Paul's instruction to 'not associate' with immoral believers provides a parallel to the discipline commanded here.

coaching.tip: 'If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat' (v. 10). Some, expecting imminent return, quit working. Paul's remedy is practical, not theoretical: hunger restores motivation. Eschatological hope never justifies present irresponsibility.

'I, Paul, write this greeting with my own hand' (v. 17). A signature authenticates—someone had forged letters (2:2). The personal touch distinguishes genuine apostolic correspondence. In an age of deception, verification matters.
